summary: Gruber examines the rampant speculation about Apple releasing a flash-memory-based iPod, arguing that the rumors fundamentally misunderstand product design by treating storage technology as a feature rather than an implementation detail. He dissects the market share statistics used to justify the rumor and points out that consumers don't choose players based on storage mechanism — they choose based on price, size, and experience. He considers three possible rationales (cheaper, smaller, sport-oriented) and finds each problematic: a cheap iPod risks the brand, a smaller one faces interface constraints, and a sport model wouldn't necessarily be cheaper. Gruber concludes that if Apple does release a flash-based iPod, the flash memory itself is the least interesting part — what matters is the purpose it serves.
key_points: Storage mechanisms are implementation details, not features — 'hard-drive player' and 'flash-memory player' are not real product categories that consumers shop by
A low-capacity, low-cost flash iPod would generate short-term sales at the expense of the iPod brand, since generous storage is central to the iPod experience
The iPod Mini was misunderstood at launch as a 'cheaper iPod' when it was actually a 'smaller iPod' — the same confusion drives flash iPod speculation
Apple has never indicated they want to corner the entire MP3 player market — just the market for good ones
The market share statistics cited by rumor promoters conflate U.S. and global numbers in misleading ways
money_quotes: Storage mechanisms are implementation details, not features.
No one goes shopping for a music player and decides against an iPod because they want a 'flash-memory player'. Many, however, decide they want a cheap music player.
The iPod isn't popular because it's cool — it's cool because it's so well-designed.
The idea that Apple can just slap the iPod brand on a low-capacity, low-cost player and 'make it work' is backwards.
Demand for lower-cost iPods exists. But, duh, demand for lower-cost everything exists. Perhaps you'd like to see a $15,000 BMW 3-series model, but you're not going to get one.
Apple has never indicated that they intend to corner the entire market for MP3 players — just the market for good ones.
No matter what the case, if there is a flash memory iPod in the pipeline, the fact that it uses flash memory is the least interesting part of the story.
Flash memory, in and of itself, is not a feature. Size, weight, and price — those are features.
key_insight: Technology choices like flash memory are implementation details, not product features — what matters is the user experience they enable, and Apple would never compromise the iPod brand for market share in a low-end segment.
